data.title: Can China and India turn tactical calm into strategic moderation?
data.description:
In recent months, India-China relations have returned to normality after a long decade of confrontation. With the resumption of direct flights after five years, recent corps commander-level talks, the reopening of the Kailash Mansarovar pilgrimage route, the relaxation of barriers on hi-tech supplies and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and President Xi Jinping exchanging pleasantries at the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO) summit, a quiet detente is under way.
This cooperation could be a product of systemic necessity driven by US pressure and tariff realignments. Unless more concrete steps are taken, it might ultimately be a pause in strategic competition.
The material logic for a pause is strong on both sides as India needs China to fulfil its industrial ambitions. Chinese electronics, electric vehicle batteries, rare earth magnets and pharmaceutical materials are essential for boosting India’s manufacturing sector. Reports suggest that Indian conglomerates such as Adani and JSW are engaging with Chinese firms over technology partnerships.
China is India’s second-largest trading partner after the United States. As Indian consumers buy many Chinese goods, bilateral trade reached about US$127.7 billion last year, out of which more than US$113 billion was Chinese exports to India.
India’s vast consumer market is an opportunity for Chinese firms facing a slowing domestic economy. Be it smartphones, cars or digital services, Chinese companies have invested heavily in Indian start-ups and are attached to India’s growing digitalisation. Last year, Indian consumers purchased almost 156 million smartphones, with Chinese brands such as Xiaomi and Vivo capturing the bulk of those sales.
India has emerged as the world’s third-largest car market, with around 4.3 million vehicles sold last year. Chinese EV giant BYD seems to believe this is its next big frontier and has publicly targeted up to a 40 per cent share of India’s EV market. Even if strategic distrust persists, the economic incentives for closer ties are huge, steering the two sides towards being more pragmatic.


The deterioration in India’s ties with the US serves as an important catalyst for this thaw. In addition to Washington imposing higher tariffs on Indian exports than on Chinese goods, the Trump administration has sharply increased fees for H-1B skilled worker visas. The US-India strategic partnership that emerged during the Obama and Biden presidencies is quickly evaporating.
The more transactional direction in India’s relationship with the US weakens its incentive to align with Washington and creates more reason to keep its diplomatic options open when it comes to China. The unravelling of US-India ties creates room for Beijing to see whether economic incentives can help in forming stable ties with New Delhi.
Both sides have started to reduce friction with calculated steps. Delhi has resumed issuing tourist visas to Chinese nationals after five years, while Beijing has relaxed restrictions on urea exports to India. Senior ministers have also started travelling again, with Foreign Minister Wang Yi visiting India after a three-year absence and Indian Foreign Secretary Subrahmanyam Jaishankar making his first trip to Beijing since 2020.
During their meeting at the SCO summit in Tianjin, Modi and Xi discussed trade balancing, people-to-people exchanges, counterterrorism and more. These small but important actions show that Beijing and Delhi are testing the waters with cooperation on low politics to establish a level of diplomatic insulation, a means of maintaining communication even in the face of hard security concerns.


It is crucial to keep in mind that China and India have already approached a solution for their disputed border. The Political Parameters document, which detailed how a final agreement could be framed, was even approved by both parties in 2005. There has not been a lack of ideas, but momentum towards a solution fell each time mistrust surged. Representatives still meet for discussions, but the border is still militarised and unresolved.
After a year-long pause, the two countries restarted talks at the corps commander level in Ladakh last month and are currently moving forward with the second phase of a three-step process for the border issue. The first stage, disengagement, has been mostly achieved; the second, de-escalation, is being addressed. The final stage will be de-induction.
For this detente to endure, it must become more than a tactical pause. Peace will only become permanent if both sides deliberately reduce the space for miscalculation. That means making the Line of Actual Control less vulnerable to accidents. Only when the border ceases to be a signalling tool and is instead governed by verifiable standards, such as patrol codes, hotline obligations and reduced troop levels, can peace be stable.
Mobility should be normalised by building constituencies that directly benefit from stability. It can be achieved through increasing flight frequency and re-establishing journalist accreditations, as well as multi-entry business and researcher visas. This can be supported by universities, think tanks and business chambers through collaborations in non-sensitive fields such as green technology.


Whether India and China decide to institutionalise caution instead of just practising it will determine the fate of this thaw. Both parties could choose to limit competition, but they are not required to give it up. With good border management, economic undertakings and mobility of common people on both sides, real progress can be made.
Managed rivalry is not a romantic notion of harmony. The realisation that both nations stand to benefit more from stability than from conflict is evident. The current calm could signal the start of a more long-lasting balance if Beijing and Delhi can turn this period of tactical tranquillity into a habit of strategic moderation.

data.title: Bangladesh’s Hasina regrets student bloodshed but slams trial as ‘judicial mockery’
data.description:
Bangladesh’s fugitive former prime minister Sheikh Hasina has expressed regret over the “needless” bloodshed as her administration tried to squash student-led protests that resulted in her losing power after 15 years, but poured scorn on a tribunal due to rule if she committed crimes against humanity by ordering the crackdown.
In a defiant interview with This Week in Asia just days before the verdict in her trial in absentia, the 78-year-old accused the interim government that replaced her of a politically motivated attack, as well as preparing to hold elections without the “consent” of millions of Bangladeshis who still support her Awami League party.
About 1,400 people died last year as state forces opened fire on student protesters after demonstrations which had begun as a rally against job quotas morphed into a violent uprising that drove Hasina and her government from office.
That ended her long stranglehold on power and forced her to take refuge in India.

Since then her regime, decried as autocratic and corrupt by many Bangladeshis, has been replaced by an interim government headed by Nobel laureate Muhammad Yunus, which has set out on ambitious reforms vowing to hold fresh elections by February.
Hasina, part of Bangladesh’s complex political history as the daughter of independence hero Sheikh Mujibur Rahman, headed the Awami League which dominated for more than a decade and steered the economy’s growth.
But as the years went by, accusations mounted of crony capitalism and authoritarianism, including extrajudicial disappearances of political rivals.
The students who drove her from Bangladesh say by the end Hasina was out of touch, insouciant to the needs of the people and surrounded by “yes men” who did her bidding in return for positions or contracts.
Speaking to This Week In Asia from India days before her much-awaited November 17 verdict on charges that her student crackdown amounted to crimes against humanity, she said the initially reasonable protests “darkened into mob violence” prompting the state actions.
“We had to act to maintain basic law and order. From there, things spiralled out of control and many people died needlessly,” Hasina said.
“I regret that very much and I would welcome a proper inquiry into what happened.”


In the months-long trial held in her absence, prosecutors in Bangladesh have alleged that Hasina ordered the use of lethal weapons to security forces against the protesters, claims she denies.
“At no point did I order security forces to fire on crowds,” she said. “My political adversaries have alleged this but have shown no persuasive evidence to back the claim, because there is none.”
Hasina has already been sentenced to six months in prison for contempt of court, and separately faces corruption charges. Other high-ranking officials from her former government also face charges, including the former interior minister – who is a fugitive – and the former chief of police, who is in custody and has already pleaded guilty.
After an initial wave of goodwill, Yunus, the interim leader, has struggled to placate Bangladesh’s competing political factions, including Islamists and student leaders who emerged from the revolution that ousted Hasina.
His administration has accused Awami League loyalists inside the civil service – as well as outside the country – of undermining reforms aimed at rooting out endemic corruption, cronyism and rebuilding the independence of the judges and police.
Bangladesh faces the prospect of more unrest as the Awami League called for a nationwide “lockdown” in protest against her trial over last year’s protests.
Schools in Dhaka and major cities across Bangladesh switched to online classes and examinations, while public transport services were severely disrupted on Thursday as the government heightened security across the country.

On Thursday, Yunus said in a televised address that his administration would hold a referendum on a national charter, which was signed last month by political parties, on the day of the next election in February. The date has not yet been announced.
The referendum, Yunus said, would propose a two-tier parliament in line with the “July National Charter” that seeks to reshape the country’s politics and institutions and give constitutional recognition to the 2024 uprising.
Yunus also repeated his pledge to punish Hasina.
Hasina, however, poured scorn on the tribunal due to rule on her case on Monday, describing it as a political hatchet job driven by her rivals. Prosecutors have called for the death penalty if she is found guilty.
“Bangladesh’s so-called International Criminal Tribunal [ICT] is neither international nor even a tribunal. It is a judicial mockery,” she said. “The ICT is controlled by my political opponents in order to deliver a preordained guilty verdict, in an attempt to nullify the Awami League as a political force.”
Despite fleeing the country, she said she was open to facing a fair trial in a “proper, impartial court”.
Hasina, who won a fourth consecutive term in 2024, said the interim government’s ban on all activities of the Awami League under the country’s Anti-Terrorism Act made a mockery of its democratic credentials ahead of next year’s election.
“An election where the Awami League, which is supported by tens of millions, is banned, is neither free, fair, nor inclusive,” she said.
“Bangladesh badly needs a government ruling with the consent of the people. You will not achieve that if millions of people are disenfranchised. Whether in government or in opposition, the Awami League needs to be part of the political conversation in Bangladesh.”

Outside looking in
From India, where she enjoys the support of Narendra Modi’s government, Hasina has launched a campaign to erode faith in the electoral process Yunus has set course on, accusing his government of exceeding its interim authority.
The South Asian nation has also been trying to stabilise the economy while negotiating emergency loans from multilateral agencies, cut public spending and reform its banking system, which it says has been drained of billions of dollars under Hasina’s time in office.
The country’s mainstay garment sector has, however, been under pressure due to higher import tariffs imposed by Washington, even as unemployment has been rampant since the Covid-19 pandemic.
The situation has been compounded by a more strained relationship with India, which has hampered Bangladesh’s logistics for exporting products such as garments.
New Delhi harbours suspicions that Dhaka may invite Chinese investment close to India’s vulnerable northeast region, as well as court Pakistan for military support.
The Yunus administration has denied trying to undermine Indian interests.

Hasina said Bangladesh needed “strong and constructive relationships” with both China and India.
Under her stewardship, Bangladesh’s economy was a success story marked by significant GDP growth and poverty reduction. But it has also been prone to weaknesses such as structural issues and institutional fragilities exacerbated by global events including the Russia-Ukraine war and the pandemic, as well as rampant corruption inside its banks.
The International Monetary Fund has projected that Bangladesh’s economy will grow by 4.9 per cent in the 2025-26 financial year, an increase from 3.8 per cent the previous year.
“These growth numbers are actually dismayingly small, versus Bangladesh’s recent historic trend – and not enough to sustain our country’s heroic efforts in recent years to lift itself out of poverty,” Hasina said.
The disgraced former leader said under the right circumstances she would return to her country, but ruled out the possibility of her party collaborating with the newly formed National Citizen Party, which has become influential due to its support base among students.
“Our role has always been to serve the people of Bangladesh, and we will continue with this mission in any way we can,” she said. “Whatever happens, we will remain peaceful.”
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Michelle Yeoh, Stray Kids, among star line-up for major K-pop award show in Hong Kong
data.description:
Fans attending Asia’s biggest K-pop awards show to be held at Hong Kong’s Kai Tak Stadium in November can expect to see Oscar-winning actress Michelle Yeoh, the global debut of a new song by South Korean boy band Stray Kids and a special collaboration with the Netflix animated series KPop Demon Hunters.
The event organisers on Wednesday announced the highlights of the 2025 MAMA Awards, featuring performances by more than 25 acts, ranging from rookie groups to K-pop veterans.
The award show, returning to Hong Kong for the first time since 2018, will take place at the city’s 50,000-seat Kai Tak Stadium, four times larger than the previous venue at the Asia-World Arena.
With over 15 awards up for grabs, the show will be held across two days, on November 28 and 29.
The sold-out event is expected to draw K-pop fans from across the world to Hong Kong, including mainland Chinese fans discussing how to travel to the venue on social media platform RedNote.
Oscar-winning actress Michelle Yeoh, renowned for her iconic roles in films such as Everything Everywhere All at Once and Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon, will appear at the event as a presenter.
South Korean actor Park Bo-gum, the host for the first evening, will perform a new interpretation of the traditional Korean song Kwaejina Ching Ching with members of boy groups BOYNEXTDOOR, CORTIS, TREASURE and dance crew BUMSUP.
The star-studded line-up on the first day includes Hong Kong boy band Mirror and South Korean groups idle, IVE, ENHYPHEN and NCT Wish, among others.
Industry veterans Super Junior will also be celebrating their 20th anniversary with a special performance.

K-pop star G-DRAGON will headline the second day of the event, which will also feature performances by aespa, CORTIS, RIIZE and Stray Kids. The latter will perform a new song from their upcoming album for the first time.
The second day will also see a special performance between host Kim Hye-soo and Felix from Stray Kids.
A special performance in collaboration with Netflix’s global hit film KPop Demon Hunters is expected to be a highlight, combining live performance with animation.
Shortlisted acts for Best Female Group include aespa, BABYMONSTER, idle, IVE, TWICE and LE SSERAFIM.
The nominations for the Best Female Artist include three members of K-pop sensation Blackpink: Jennie, Jisoo and Rosé.
Fans who are unable to get their hands on tickets to the sold-out show can watch the live stream via Mnet Plus.

data.title: China’s US$8 billion power bank giant Anker seeks share listing in Hong Kong
data.description:
Chinese maker of portable charging devices Anker Innovations, which went viral last year as the preferred power bank of US President Donald Trump, plans to list in Hong Kong amid US scrutiny and recent product recalls.
Based in Changsha, the capital of central China’s Hunan province, Shenzhen-listed Anker will apply to offer its shares on the main board of the Hong Kong stock exchange, it said in a filing with the Shenzhen bourse, on Wednesday.
The move aimed to “further advance the company’s global strategies” and improve its “comprehensive competitiveness”, it said.
With a current market capitalisation of 58.9 billion yuan (US$8 billion), Anker is one of the most popular power bank brands on Amazon and other international e-commerce sites. The company garnered global attention on social media when Trump was seen using an Anker power bank during a live-streamed conversation with Tesla CEO Elon Musk in 2024.
The overseas market accounted for over 96.4 per cent of Anker’s total sales in 2024, according to its annual report.
However, its global ambitions face the pressure of US scrutiny after a lawmaker called for an inquiry into Anker for alleged tariff evasion and potential abuse of trade in September.
Anker’s Hong Kong listing plan comes after the company initiated multiple rounds of product recalls at home and abroad.
Last month, Anker voluntarily recalled 410,000 mobile battery units and 110,000 lithium-ion battery-powered speakers in Japan, citing potential fire risks.
In June, the company recalled more than 1.1 million power banks in the US over concerns that their lithium-ion batteries could overheat, “posing fire and burn hazards to consumers”, the US Consumer Product Safety Commission said.
In the same month, Anker initiated a recall in China covering more than 712,000 units across seven power bank models.
Anker was founded by former Google engineer Steven Yang Meng in 2011. Yang, a Peking University graduate, quit his job at Google to build Anker with his mother’s life savings and less than US$1 million in funding. He serves as CEO.
The company has since grown to more than 5,000 employees globally, with business in over 146 countries and regions, it said.
Anker announced its ambition to make humanoid robotics this year, saying it would start with robots for household uses, such as vacuum cleaners and lawn mowers.
The company’s charging and energy-storage businesses – the former includes power banks and the latter outdoor power-station products – accounted for half of its total revenue last year. Smart home items and audio products made up the rest.
Anker’s revenue grew 41 per cent year-on-year in 2024 to 24.7 billion yuan. Net income rose 31 per cent to 2.1 billion yuan.
The company’s Shenzhen shares gained 0.5 per cent to 110.21 yuan on Thursday.

data.title: US invites KMT chief Cheng Li-wun to Washington, stresses priority to ‘avoid war’
data.description:
The head of the de facto US embassy in Taiwan has invited new Kuomintang (KMT) chairwoman Cheng Li-wun to visit the United States, stressing that avoiding war is the top priority.
The invitation from Raymond Greene, director of the American Institute in Taiwan (AIT), came during an in-person meeting at the KMT headquarters in Taipei on Wednesday, according to a statement from Taiwan’s largest opposition party.
The KMT – considered friendlier to Beijing than the ruling Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) – holds a slim majority in the legislature with the island’s other opposition party, the Taiwan People’s Party.
“The US has never sought conflict across the Taiwan Strait. The primary goal is to avoid war and ensure that any cross-strait differences are handled peacefully and without coercion,” Greene said.

His remarks came just two months after a comment from the institute publicly challenging Beijing’s position on Taiwan.
In September, a spokesman for the AIT drew fierce opposition from Beijing by saying that Taiwan’s international status remained “undetermined”.
In Beijing, foreign ministry spokesman Lin Jian said the remark “sent a gravely wrong signal to the Taiwan independence separatist forces”.
Wednesday’s meeting also came just two weeks after a summit between Chinese President Xi Jinping and his US counterpart, Donald Trump, in Busan.
In a clear break from past practice, the two leaders did not discuss Taiwan during their face-to-face meeting, a sign some experts said suggested a tactical agreement on the issue between the two sides.
US Secretary of Defence Pete Hegseth has also signalled repeatedly in his conversations with Chinese Defence Minister Dong Jun that Washington does not seek conflict with Beijing.
The AIT has in the past invited new KMT chairs to the US but the invitation for Cheng has stood out. William Lai Ching-te, the island’s leader, has so far not secured his own transit stop in the US as his predecessors did early in their terms.
Lai’s two most recent predecessors – Ma Ying-jeou and Tsai Ing-wen – transited through the US within two weeks and two months of taking office, respectively.
Washington requested in July that Lai cancel transit arrangements that had been made, according to The New York Times.
During Wednesday’s meeting, Cheng said that only through stable communication and mutual trust can regional peace and stability be jointly maintained, adding: “Taiwan-US relations are crucial, and we hope the United States will continue to provide understanding and support.”
The KMT statement said that both sides had agreed that any changes to the status quo on the Taiwan Strait should respect the wishes of people on both sides of the Taiwan Strait.
Cheng, 55, was elected the new chairwoman of the KMT last month. A former street activist, she previously served in the independence-leaning DPP, but her political stance is now considered more pro-Beijing than those of her predecessors.
Cheng has bluntly declared that she hopes “every Taiwanese person can proudly say they are Chinese”. Analysts, however, have said Cheng’s views are not considered mainstream in Taiwan.
Before being elected chairwoman, Cheng had voiced scepticism about US defence help to Taiwan. The US is the island’s main arms supplier.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunified by force if necessary.
Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise the self-ruled island as an independent state. However, Washington is opposed to any attempt to seize Taiwan by force and is legally bound to supply the island with weapons.

data.title: Teen who promoted ‘Hong Kong Parliament’ jailed for 1 year for sedition
data.description:
A Hong Kong court has sentenced a 19-year-old woman to a year in prison for promoting a self-styled parliament-in-exile in Canada in breach of the domestic national security law.
West Kowloon Court on Thursday sentenced Bettie Lan Fei on a count of sedition for rallying support for the election of a so-called Hong Kong Parliament that sought to topple the city government and eliminate the Communist Party of China.
Chief Magistrate Victor So Wai-tak said the defendant had contributed materially to the dissemination of the political group’s radical and ill-conceived objectives by filming two promotional videos and encouraging a friend to vote in the election.
So, who is among the few jurists hand-picked by the city leader to adjudicate national security proceedings, also weighed in on the group’s illegality, saying it inflamed opposition to the Communist Party’s sovereignty and authority over Hong Kong and provoked lasting hostility towards the regime.
“The entity concerned was established on a foreign basis, with the purpose of propagating hostile sentiments and opposition to the People’s Republic of China on a global scale,” he said.
“The entire operation was designed to disseminate distorted portrayals of Chinese sovereignty and of the [Hong Kong Special Administrative Region] within the international community, to malign the Chinese authority and to mislead foreign states and their citizens into accepting extremist ideology.”
In a hearing last month, the court heard how Lan’s ex-boyfriend, wanted activist Tony Lam, convinced her to appear in two videos promoting the Canada-based organisation’s poll for a C$200 (US$143) reward.
National security police have issued HK$200,000 (US$25,700) bounties for 15 overseas Hongkongers for their involvement in the group, including 36-year-old Lam, who ran in the poll and was sworn in as a so-called parliament member.
The force has also offered bounties of HK$1 million each for the arrest of four activists who founded the pseudo-legislature – Elmer Yuan Gong-yi, Victor Ho Leung-mau, Johnny Fok Ka-chi and Tony Choi Ming-da.
In two promotional videos dated April and May this year, Lan, who was portrayed as a new-generation Hongkonger, said the group would fight for residents’ rights and help their jailed “comrades”, while calling on the audience to cast a vote via a mobile app.
Lan also encouraged a friend to take part in the vote in private messages on Instagram, in which she described the so-called parliament as an overseas regime.
The defendant pleaded guilty to a count of “doing with a seditious intention an act or acts that had a seditious intention”, which is punishable by up to seven years in jail.
A probation officer from the Social Welfare Department said that Lan’s offence might have stemmed from her emotional dependence on her former partner, her naivety, and her failure to understand the legal consequences of her actions.
Defence counsel Luke McGuinniety on Thursday said his client was “quite terrified” by the inevitable custodial sentence, adding that she looked forward to turning the page upon her release.

In sentencing, So noted that Lan had subscribed to the so-called parliament’s beliefs, as evidenced in social media posts that showed her association with election candidates and endorsement of “unfounded allegations” of persecution in Xinjiang, Hong Kong and mainland China.
But the magistrate found the accused was to some extent vulnerable to the undue influence of her much older and more mature ex-boyfriend.
The court reduced the starting sentence of 1½ years by a third to reflect Lan’s timely plea.
Six men were previously jailed for between 10 and 14 months for sedition in breach of the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, which was enacted in accordance with a requirement under Article 23 of the Basic Law, the city’s mini-constitution.
Separately, the father of wanted activist Anna Kwok Fung-yee is standing trial under the Article 23 law for allegedly handling her financial assets by attempting to withdraw cash from a life and personal accident insurance policy under her name. The trial will resume next month.

data.title: Tesla, Volvo and Mercedes-Benz win China’s approval to deploy AI chatbots in cars
data.description:
Tesla, Volvo and Mercedes-Benz have become the first foreign carmakers approved to deploy artificial intelligence chatbots in their vehicles in China, marking a potential acceleration of Beijing’s vetting process for AI services.
The Cyberspace Administration of Beijing said in a notice that it registered the “Mercedes-Benz virtual assistant” as a generative AI service on Tuesday, the same day the Shanghai cyberspace administration approved Tesla Shanghai’s xBot service and Volvo’s local chatbot Xiao Wo.
The approvals come as AI becomes increasingly embedded in hardware products, from electric vehicles to smartphones. China implemented a registration-based system for generative AI services in April 2024, requiring all such services to be officially registered before they can be offered to consumers.
The registration – a de facto examination and approval process – serves as a key regulatory requirement for foreign firms seeking to launch AI services in China. Apple has yet to receive approval to roll out its Apple Intelligence into iPhones in mainland China, while Tesla is waiting for the go-ahead to deploy its Full Self-Driving (FSD) software locally.
Domestic brands have been rushing to register their own AI services to gain a competitive edge over foreign brands.
According to the Cyberspace Administration of China, 611 generative AI services had been registered as of November 1. The cyberspace administration of Beijing said it had registered 183 generative AI services as of Wednesday, while Shanghai had registered 115.
The approvals for foreign carmakers offer hopes that China may be streamlining its review process.
Tesla CEO Elon Musk said last week that he expected its FSD to be fully approved in China by early next year.
“We have partial approval in China, and hopefully we’ll have full approval around February or March,” Musk said at Tesla’s annual general meeting on Thursday.
Tesla said in August it was rolling out an upgraded voice-assistant system for its vehicles in mainland China, integrating AI from DeepSeek and ByteDance to better engage customers in the world’s largest automotive market.
As of mid-February, more than a dozen domestic carmakers – including BYD, Geely and Stellantis-backed start-up Leapmotor – had already announced plans to release cars with DeepSeek-enabled AI features.

data.title: Beijing vows to strike back if Japan uses military force in Taiwan Strait
data.description:
Beijing has warned that any Japanese military involvement in the Taiwan Strait would be regarded as “an act of aggression” and vowed to strike back in response, marking the latest twist in a diplomatic spat over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on Taiwan.
The Chinese foreign ministry made the comments on Thursday – just days after Beijing lodged a protest over Takaichi’s remarks last week that Japan could deploy its military in the event of a cross-strait conflict.
“If Japan dares to use military force to intervene in the Taiwan Strait, that will constitute an act of aggression, and China will strike back forcefully,” foreign ministry spokesman Lin Jian said.
“We will resolutely exercise the right of self-defence afforded by the United Nations Charter and international law and steadfastly defend our national sovereignty and territorial integrity.”
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including Japan and the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.


Takaichi told the Japanese parliament last week that the use of military force in the Taiwan Strait could be seen as a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan.
Under the country’s 2015 security law, a “survival-threatening situation” means that an armed attack on Tokyo’s allies poses an existential threat to Japan, allowing it to deploy its self-defence forces.
Takaichi later clarified that her remarks were only “hypothetical” and that she would refrain from making similar comments again. But she declined to retract her remarks.
“China firmly opposes this and will not tolerate it. Japan must immediately correct itself and retract those hostile remarks. Otherwise, Japan must bear all the consequences,” Lin said.
“In its militaristic history, Japan repeatedly launched foreign aggression under the pretext of so-called existential crises, including instigating the war of aggression against China under the guise of exercising the right of self-defence,” he said.
“Now that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi has again raised the so-called existential crisis scenario, what is her intent? Is she trying to repeat the militarist path of the past?” Lin added.
“Does she intend to make enemies again of the Chinese people and the peoples of Asia? Is she attempting to overturn the post-war international order?”
This year marks the 80th anniversary of China’s victory over Japan in World War II and Japan’s surrender of sovereignty over Taiwan.
Lin reiterated that the Taiwan issue “must not be interfered with by any external force”, and an attempt to intervene would be an open provocation against the post-war international order and seriously damage Sino-Japanese relations.
“Do not play with fire on the Taiwan issue – those who play with fire will get burned,” he said.

data.title: Why listening can heal, connect, even transform relationships, as long as you do it right
data.description:
In an age punctuated by constant chatter and phone notifications, Japanese-American author Haru Yamada hopes to get people to do something radical: just to listen.
For London-based Yamada, a sociolinguist who studies how society and culture shape language use, the idea grew not only from decades of research but from an experience that brought her close to silence.
She was in the intensive care unit of a London hospital recovering from an accident that was going to take away her ability to hear, when she learned to really listen.
“I was able to heal not only through the medical interventions of surgeries, lung drains, IVs and artificial ventilators, but also through the support of listeners,” she says.
“The light-bulb moment came when I realised that the benefit of listening lies not only in receiving information, but in the energy of connection we experience when we witness one another in relationship.”

This sparked the idea for her new book, Kiku: The Japanese Art of Good Listening, which explores how to listen – kiku, in Japanese – can heal, connect and transform relationships.
“My structured training in listening began in the field of linguistics, which traditionally looked at the structural forms of language and defined itself as a ‘science’,” Yamada says.
“Listening as an aesthetic, stylistic and sometimes performative art form takes this a step further, suggesting that listening isn’t merely a component of language used to receive information, but part of our shared culture, an invitation to share space, to witness one another, and to make each other feel seen and heard.”
One of the biggest misconceptions about listening is that it can refer to obeying, as in “Listen to me!”
It is so much more than that, though. She outlines 14 different “channels” of listening, including informational listening, which we use to gain information, and “relational listening”, in which we can hear a person’s emotion, culture and willingness to relate.
“One common but unfortunate misunderstanding is that listening means following instruction, the kind of listening adults often invoke when they accuse children of not listening,” she says.
“Listening isn’t just auditory. It isn’t just words assembled by grammar. Listening draws on all the senses available to us in a shared space. It’s visual and tactile, as well as verbal.
“And equally, although often misunderstood, listening isn’t merely ‘non-verbal communication’ either. Deep listening is powerful because it engages all our listening energies at once.”

In the digital age, where conversation is constant but attention is scarce, Yamada takes a nuanced view.
Has modern communication made us worse listeners? “Yes and no,” she replies.
“The cacophony of social media, where everyone is invited to speak, has simultaneously encouraged people to talk more and pushed listeners to retreat into isolated echo chambers.
“Yet these same pressures have also created new modes of communication, such as texting, often with emojis, that can foster intimacy where it was previously absent.
“Just as language evolves, our speaking and listening adapt to the times.”
For those who want to listen better, Yamada offers starting points.
“Being a good listener is a practice we can weave into daily life,” she says. “We can discover which listening channels we tend to turn on by habit. Like the instructions for applying an oxygen mask in an emergency landing, we must first understand our own listening practice before we can use it effectively.
“When someone speaks, they extend their person space and invite you to join them. When we decide to listen, we accept that invitation. When we decide not to, we decline it. Becoming aware of the spaces we’re invited into, and choosing which ones to join, is part of listening.”

Silence, Yamada says, is not the absence of sound but the space where meaning is given a chance to take hold.
“Silence is the space where listening can unfold. Rather than emptiness, silence offers room for the speaker’s words and what they leave unsaid to resonate fully.”
Ultimately, the book is a cultural study and a reminder of how something as simple as listening can restore connection in a divided world.
“In today’s political climate, we often stumble in one of two ways, either by debating ideas without appreciating the people behind them, or by focusing on identity while overlooking the context that gives those ideas meaning,” Yamada says.
“If instead we adjust our listening channels to include both what people say and who they are, we can create spaces of overlapping perspective where connection becomes possible.”
Kiku urges us to slow down and listen with full attention.
“Listening,” Yamada says, “is a quiet act of turning distance into understanding. Whether in friendship or across cultures, listening interrupts the instinct to persuade and opens a space for empathy, repair and trust to grow. Every act of listening, however small, is a quiet act of repair in a noisy world.”
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: How has Hong Kong-Singapore Asian Cup football qualifier rekindled scalping issue?
data.description:
The highly anticipated Asian Cup qualifying match between Hong Kong and Singapore at Kai Tak Stadium next Tuesday is the latest mega-event in the city targeted by scalpers, some of whom were found reselling tickets at 17 times the official price.
Disappointed fans found that the match sold out in less than 80 minutes last Friday, but discovered listings on sites including online marketplace Carousell. One seller offered three tickets in the upper-level fan zone for HK$4,000 (US$515) – nearly 17 times the original total price of HK$240.
Hong Kong’s legislation on the unauthorised resale of tickets, the Places of Public Entertainment Ordinance, was introduced decades ago to deal with gangs who touted tickets in person, but fails to regulate today’s predominant system of online sales.
The Post takes a look at how the critical game triggered a fresh round of ticket scalping in Hong Kong.
What is the outcry about?
Tickets for the match sold out in less than 80 minutes last Friday after going on sale at 3pm via the online platform Ticketflap, with no real-name verification required.
Customers could buy up to six tickets in each transaction.
Fans were further angered when listings appeared on sites including online marketplace Carousell, raising concerns that scalpers had snapped up large numbers of tickets.
One scalper offered three tickets in the upper-level fan zone for HK$4,000 (US$515) – nearly 17 times the original total price of HK$240.
Supporters flooded the Hong Kong Football Association’s (HKFA) social media pages to complain about being unable to buy tickets, with some calling the arrangement “chaotic” and “unfair”.
Why is this match important?
Victory would send the Hong Kong team to the 2027 AFC Asian Cup finals in Saudi Arabia, while defeat would end their qualification hopes.
A draw would mean both teams having to fight on in a final round of qualifiers next March.
The HKFA on Wednesday upped the stakes by offering the team a HK$1 million (US$128,700) “reward” if they defeated Singapore.

What are the developments so far?
Following the backlash, HKFA chief executive Charles Cheung Yim-yau said on Thursday it was “not their responsibility” to police ticket sales.
He said that the speed at which the game had sold out meant “our matches are very popular”.
Cheung added that HKFA listened to fans’ feedback, citing the fact that each customer could only buy six tickets for the Singapore match, after complaints over people being able to buy eight for the last qualifier against Bangladesh in October.
Faced with sky-high demand, some Hong Kong fans registered through the Football Association of Singapore (FAS) to secure seats through its ticketing partner Ticketek in the away fans’ area, saying it was the only way they could get a ticket.
In response to media queries, an FAS spokesman said it is aware of recent reports on the “resale and misuse of away tickets”.
The FAS said it would require fans to show a valid Singapore photo ID or passport that matches the name of the purchaser during ticket collection.
Ticket holders would be required to sign an “on-site fan declaration” that they would support Singapore only and refrain from wearing home team apparel or displaying home team paraphernalia.
Is scalping illegal in Hong Kong?
Under the Public Entertainment Ordinance, it is an offence to offer or sell tickets at higher prices than those fixed by organisers at licensed venues. The unauthorised sale of tickets – even at official prices – is also outlawed “in any public thoroughfare, or in the entrance hall of, or approaches to” the venue.
Offenders face a fine of up to HK$2,000 - a penalty that has remained unchanged since 1950.
But while criminalising in-person touting at entertainment venues, the law does not cover online scalping, which has plagued the city in an age of internet sales.
The Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau previously told the legislature it was studying possible measures including new legislation to criminalise ticket scalping for events held at Leisure and Cultural Services Department venues.
But it maintained that “legislative control has its pros and cons”, given the multiple causes of scalping, including supply and demand.
Why is there a market for scalpers?
In 2019, the department announced that organisers of events with more than four shows at its venues must limit internal and priority sales to no more than 70 per cent, leaving only 30 per cent for public sales.
While venues managed by the government must follow the guidelines, private ones such as those at Kai Tak Sports Park and AsiaWorld-Expo can decide their own sales terms.
Event industry insiders told the Post earlier that commercial interests meant that organisers were contractually obliged to reserve up to 60 per cent of tickets for their sponsors’ internal distribution or sales through other channels as part of their contract.
Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui previously said her bureau had met several major organisers to understand their perspectives.
But she noted that events were commercial activities and “need to be financially viable”, while large-scale productions required a certain level of sponsorship.
“It is not something government action can easily change. As long as it’s not illegal, the government’s hand should not be too far-reaching as Hong Kong is a free market,” she said.

Is real name registration a solution?
The Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens, which welcomed almost 50,000 fans on each of its three days this year, uses real-name registration for ticket sales.
The system has also been deployed for the ongoing National Games, in which Hong Kong is staging events in eight sports.
In response to the outrage from football fans, Cheung, the HKFA CEO, defended his organisation’s sales mechanism, saying it did not need to apologise for the backlash and that it was “not their responsibility” to police ticket sales.
Cheung said having to verify fans’ identity on match night could cause delays, and that after “cautious consideration”, the HKFA had decided that real-name registration risked “creating other big issues”.
But he acknowledged that there were no measures in place to stop unscrupulous people from buying tickets and reselling them at inflated prices.
Culture, sports and tourism minister Law previously said that while real-name ticketing was a proven solution, the government acknowledged that many event organisers were reluctant to adopt the system out of business concerns.
